passage.text:
IELTS Reading Practice
Urbanisation & Society
10 Reading Passages  ·  500–600 words each  ·  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)

Contents
Passage 1: The Growth of Cities — Why People Move to Urban Areas
Passage 2: Public Transport — Moving People Around Cities
Passage 3: Housing — The Challenge of Affordable Homes
Passage 4: Smart Cities — Using Technology to Improve Urban Life
Passage 5: Migration — People on the Move
Passage 6: Ageing Populations — A Challenge for Modern Society
Passage 7: Crime and Safety in Cities
Passage 8: Waste Management — Dealing with What We Throw Away
Passage 9: Gender Equality — Progress and Challenges
Passage 10: Social Media and Society — Connecting or Dividing Us?

Passage 1: The Growth of Cities — Why People Move to Urban Areas
Topic: Urbanisation  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Urbanisation is the process by which more and more people move from the countryside to towns and cities. It is one of the most significant trends in the modern world. For most of human history, the majority of people lived in rural areas — in small villages and farming communities. This began to change during the Industrial Revolution in the 18th and 19th centuries, when factories were built in cities and people moved there to find work. Today, more than half of the world's population — over four billion people — lives in urban areas. The United Nations estimates that by 2050, this figure could rise to about two thirds of the global population. The growth of cities is especially rapid in developing countries in Asia and Africa.
Paragraph B
There are many reasons why people choose to move to cities. The most common reason is the search for better economic opportunities. Cities generally have more jobs available than rural areas, and those jobs tend to pay better. Many factories, offices, shops, and businesses are based in cities, creating employment for large numbers of people. Cities also offer better access to services such as hospitals, schools, universities, and shops — things that may not be available or easily accessible in rural areas. For young people in particular, cities can be exciting places that offer more choices for education, entertainment, and social life. Many people also move to cities to join family members or friends who have already moved there.
Paragraph C
Urbanisation brings many benefits, both for the people who move and for the wider economy. When people move to cities, they often have access to better education and healthcare, which improves their quality of life. Cities bring together large numbers of people with different skills and ideas, which can drive creativity, innovation, and economic growth. Many of the world's most important companies, universities, and research centres are located in cities. Historically, urbanisation has been closely linked to economic development — as countries have become more urbanised, they have generally also become wealthier. Well-planned cities can also be more environmentally efficient than rural areas, because people living close together can share resources and public transport more easily.
Paragraph D
However, rapid urbanisation also creates serious problems. Many cities, especially in developing countries, have grown faster than their infrastructure — their roads, water systems, housing, and public services — can keep up with. This leads to overcrowding, traffic congestion, a shortage of affordable housing, and insufficient public transport. Informal settlements — sometimes called "slums" — develop when poor newcomers cannot afford proper housing and build their own homes from basic materials in areas without clean water or sanitation. Today, around one billion people around the world live in such settlements. Pollution — both air and water — is also a major problem in many rapidly growing cities. Managing the challenges of urban growth is one of the most important tasks for city planners and governments around the world.

Passage 2: Public Transport — Moving People Around Cities
Topic: Transport & Urban Planning  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
One of the biggest challenges that cities face is how to move large numbers of people around efficiently. As cities grow, more and more people need to travel to work, school, shops, and other destinations every day. If most of these people use private cars, the result is serious traffic congestion — long queues of vehicles that move very slowly and waste enormous amounts of time and fuel. Air pollution from car engines also becomes a major health problem. Public transport — buses, trains, underground railways (or "metros"), trams, and ferries — offers a solution by moving many people in the same vehicle, reducing the total number of vehicles on the road and the amount of pollution they produce.
Paragraph B
Good public transport systems bring many benefits to cities and their residents. They reduce traffic congestion, making journeys faster for everyone — even those who still use cars. They reduce air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions, which is important for both public health and the environment. Public transport also makes cities more equal — it gives people who cannot afford a car, including the elderly, young people, and those on lower incomes, the ability to travel easily and access jobs, education, and services. In cities with excellent public transport, people often choose not to own a car at all, saving significant amounts of money on purchase, fuel, insurance, and maintenance.
Paragraph C
Some cities around the world have built particularly impressive public transport systems. Tokyo in Japan has one of the most extensive and efficient metro systems in the world, carrying millions of passengers every day with remarkable punctuality — trains are often on time to within seconds. Singapore has invested heavily in its Mass Rapid Transit system and bus network, making it easy and affordable to travel anywhere in the city without a car. In Europe, cities like Amsterdam and Copenhagen have invested not only in public transport but also in cycling infrastructure, making cycling a safe, pleasant, and very popular way to travel. Bogotá in Colombia has built a successful Bus Rapid Transit system — dedicated lanes for high-capacity buses — which has transformed transport in the city at a fraction of the cost of building a metro.
Paragraph D
Despite its many benefits, building and maintaining good public transport is expensive and challenging. Underground metro systems cost billions of dollars to build and can take many years to complete. In many cities, particularly in developing countries, the existing transport infrastructure is insufficient for the growing population, but governments do not have enough money to upgrade it quickly. Another challenge is encouraging people to use public transport instead of their cars. Many people prefer the comfort and convenience of driving their own vehicle, especially if public transport is slow, crowded, or unreliable. Experts suggest that the most effective approach is to both improve public transport and make driving less attractive — for example, through congestion charges (fees for driving in busy city centres) and reduced parking spaces.

Passage 3: Housing — The Challenge of Affordable Homes
Topic: Housing & Urban Society  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Affordable housing is one of the most pressing problems facing cities around the world. In many large cities — including London, New York, Sydney, and Hong Kong — the cost of buying or renting a home has risen so much that ordinary workers find it very difficult to afford to live anywhere near where they work. The problem is especially serious for young people, who often spend a very large proportion of their income on rent and find it almost impossible to save enough money to buy a home of their own. In the most expensive cities in the world, a typical home may cost fifteen, twenty, or even thirty times the average annual salary. This means that for many people, owning a home has become an unachievable dream.
Paragraph B
There are several reasons why housing costs have risen so much in many cities. One important factor is that the supply of new homes has not kept up with the growing demand. In many cities, strict rules about where and what kind of buildings can be constructed make it difficult and slow to build new housing. At the same time, the population of cities has grown rapidly as more people move in from rural areas or from other countries. Another factor is investment — wealthy individuals and companies sometimes buy large numbers of properties as financial investments rather than to live in, which drives up prices and reduces the number of homes available for people who actually need somewhere to live.
Paragraph C
The shortage of affordable housing has many negative consequences for society. When ordinary workers cannot afford to live near their workplaces, they must either commute very long distances — which wastes time, is tiring, and adds to traffic and pollution — or they must live in overcrowded conditions, sharing small flats with many other people. High housing costs can trap people in poverty by leaving them with very little money for food, healthcare, and other essentials after paying rent. Homelessness — when people cannot afford any kind of home — is a growing problem in many wealthy cities. High housing costs also reduce social diversity in city centres, as only wealthy people can afford to live there, pushing lower-income residents to the outer areas of cities.
Paragraph D
Governments use several different approaches to try to make housing more affordable. Social housing — homes built and owned by the government or housing associations and rented at below-market rates to people on lower incomes — is an important tool in many countries. In countries like Singapore, the government has built large amounts of public housing that is available to the majority of the population at affordable prices. Financial support — such as help-to-buy schemes that help first-time buyers save for a deposit — is another approach. Planning reform — changing the rules to allow more homes to be built more quickly — is also widely recommended. Some cities have introduced rent controls — limits on how much landlords are allowed to charge — though economists debate whether these are effective in the long term.

Passage 4: Smart Cities — Using Technology to Improve Urban Life
Topic: Technology & Urban Planning  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
A "smart city" is a city that uses digital technology and data to improve the way it works and the quality of life of its residents. The idea is to connect different parts of the city — its transport systems, energy supply, water systems, waste management, and public services — through the internet and sensors, so that they can share information and work more efficiently. Smart cities use the data collected to make better decisions — for example, adjusting traffic lights in real time to reduce congestion, or detecting water pipe leaks before they become serious problems. As cities around the world grow larger and more complex, many city governments are turning to technology to help manage them more effectively.
Paragraph B
There are already many examples of smart city technology in use around the world. In Singapore, a national sensor network monitors everything from traffic and air quality to the water level in reservoirs. The data collected is analysed and used to help plan and manage the city more efficiently. In Barcelona, smart street lighting automatically adjusts its brightness depending on whether anyone is nearby, saving significant amounts of electricity. Smart bins in some cities send a signal when they are full, so that waste collection vehicles only visit when needed, saving fuel and reducing unnecessary journeys. In Estonia, most government services — including voting, filing taxes, and registering a business — can be done completely online, saving citizens enormous amounts of time.
Paragraph C
Smart city technology has the potential to bring many benefits. Better traffic management can reduce commuting times and fuel consumption, cutting both costs and pollution. Smart energy grids can balance supply and demand more efficiently, reducing energy waste. Real-time information on public transport — such as an app showing exactly when the next bus will arrive — can make it easier and more attractive for people to use public transport instead of driving. Smart city systems can also make it faster and easier for residents to access government services, report problems like broken street lights or damaged roads, and receive information in emergencies. In the long term, smarter management of city resources could save governments billions of dollars.
Paragraph D
However, smart cities also raise important concerns. The collection of large amounts of data about people's movements, behaviour, and daily routines raises serious questions about privacy. Who has access to this data, and how is it protected? Could it be used by governments to monitor and control their citizens? The more dependent a city becomes on digital systems, the more vulnerable it is to cyberattacks — if a city's transport or energy systems are hacked, the consequences could be very serious. There is also a risk that smart city technology benefits wealthier areas and more tech-savvy residents more than poorer communities, increasing rather than reducing inequality within cities. Experts agree that to be truly "smart," cities must ensure that the benefits of technology are shared fairly and that residents' privacy and rights are protected.

Passage 5: Migration — People on the Move
Topic: Migration & Society  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Migration is the movement of people from one place to another, either within a country or between countries. It has been a part of human life throughout history — people have always moved in search of better conditions, escaping danger, or following opportunities. Today, migration is a major global phenomenon. According to the United Nations, there are around 280 million international migrants in the world — people living in a country that is not the country where they were born. This is about 3.6% of the world's total population. When we include people who move within their own country — for example, from a rural area to a city — the numbers are much larger. Migration affects individuals, families, communities, and whole nations in profound ways.
Paragraph B
People migrate for many different reasons. Economic migration is the most common type — people move to find work, earn more money, or improve their standard of living. This often involves moving from poorer to wealthier countries, or from rural to urban areas. Refugees are people who are forced to leave their homes because of war, persecution, or natural disasters. They are legally protected under international law and have the right to seek safety in other countries. Family reunification — moving to join family members who have already migrated — is another very common reason for migration. Some people also migrate for education — to study at universities or schools in other countries. In recent years, climate change has begun to force people to move as floods, droughts, and rising sea levels make some areas uninhabitable.
Paragraph C
Migration brings important benefits to both the countries people move to and the countries they leave. In receiving countries, migrants fill important gaps in the labour market — they often do jobs that local people are not available or willing to do, in sectors like healthcare, construction, agriculture, and hospitality. Migrants also pay taxes and contribute to the economy in many other ways, including starting businesses and creating jobs. Many countries with ageing populations depend on younger migrants to support their health and social care systems. For sending countries, migrants often send money — called "remittances" — back to their families at home. Remittances are a major source of income for many developing countries, often exceeding the amount of foreign aid they receive.
Paragraph D
Migration can also create challenges and tensions. In some receiving countries, there are concerns that migrants take jobs from local people or put pressure on public services such as schools and hospitals. Research generally shows that these concerns are exaggerated, and that migrants contribute more to the economy than they take out. However, migration can change the cultural composition of communities quickly, and some residents feel uncomfortable with rapid change. Migrants themselves often face significant difficulties — learning a new language, adapting to a different culture, finding work and housing, and dealing with discrimination and prejudice. Managing migration in a way that is fair to both migrants and host communities, and that makes the most of the economic and social benefits migration brings, is one of the great challenges of the modern world.

Passage 6: Ageing Populations — A Challenge for Modern Society
Topic: Demographics & Society  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
One of the most significant changes happening in many countries today is the ageing of the population. In many developed countries — including Japan, Germany, Italy, and South Korea — the proportion of elderly people in the population is increasing rapidly, while the birth rate is falling. This means that there are fewer young people to support a growing number of older people. In Japan, for example, around 30% of the population is already over the age of 65 — the highest proportion of elderly people of any country in the world. Japan's total population has already started to shrink. Other wealthy nations are following a similar pattern, though usually at a slower pace. This demographic shift — a change in the age structure of the population — has very significant consequences for society and the economy.
Paragraph B
Ageing populations create serious challenges for healthcare and social care systems. Older people generally require more medical care than younger people, and as the proportion of elderly people grows, the demand for hospital care, medicines, nursing homes, and home care services increases significantly. At the same time, as the proportion of working-age people falls, there are fewer people paying the taxes that fund these services. Many governments are worried about how they will afford to provide adequate healthcare and pensions for a growing elderly population in the future. Some countries have already started to raise the retirement age — the age at which people become entitled to receive a government pension — in order to keep people in work for longer and reduce the cost of pension payments.
Paragraph C
Ageing populations also affect the economy in other ways. As the working-age population shrinks, there may be fewer workers available to fill jobs, which can slow economic growth. Some industries that depend on physical labour — such as construction and farming — may face particular difficulties finding enough workers. Companies may find it harder to find the skilled staff they need. Some economists argue that ageing societies need to become more innovative and productive — using technology and automation to compensate for the smaller workforce. Immigration is also often proposed as a solution — bringing in younger workers from other countries can help balance an ageing population and fill gaps in the labour market, though this is often a politically sensitive issue.
Paragraph D
It is also important to recognise that older people are a positive resource for society, not simply a burden. Many older people remain healthy, active, and mentally sharp well into their 70s and 80s. They contribute to society in many ways — through paid work, voluntary activities, and by providing childcare for grandchildren, which helps parents return to the workforce. Older people represent a significant market for businesses — they often have more savings and time to spend on travel, leisure, and other goods and services. The key challenge is to build societies that are genuinely "age-friendly" — that respect the dignity and contributions of older people, keep them socially connected and mentally stimulated, and make good use of the experience and knowledge they have accumulated over their lifetimes.

Passage 7: Crime and Safety in Cities
Topic: Urban Society & Security  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Crime is a concern for people living in cities around the world. While many cities are very safe, others struggle with high rates of theft, violent crime, or drug-related offences. The feeling of safety — or lack of it — has a big impact on people's quality of life and on how willingly they use public spaces, walk at night, or allow their children to play outside. Cities with high crime rates can struggle to attract businesses and tourists, which can hold back economic development. Understanding what causes crime and what makes some cities safer than others is an important question for governments, city planners, and communities.
Paragraph B
Research suggests that crime is closely linked to social and economic conditions. Cities and neighbourhoods with high levels of poverty, unemployment, and inequality tend to have higher crime rates. When people have very little and see others with a great deal, some may turn to crime out of desperation or frustration. A lack of opportunities — for education, employment, and positive social activities — can also push young people towards criminal behaviour. Poor housing conditions, overcrowding, and a lack of community facilities can make it harder for people to live well and lead to social tensions. This suggests that reducing crime in the long term requires not just better policing, but also investment in education, housing, jobs, and community services.
Paragraph C
Many cities have used a variety of approaches to improve safety. Increasing the number of police officers on the streets — particularly on foot patrol in high-crime areas — can deter crime and make residents feel safer. Improved street lighting makes it less easy for criminals to operate unseen. Closed-circuit television cameras (CCTV) are used in many cities to monitor public spaces and help police identify suspects after crimes have taken place. Community policing — where police officers build relationships with the communities they serve, rather than simply responding to crimes after they happen — has been shown to be effective in reducing crime and improving trust between police and residents. Some cities have had success with youth programmes that offer young people at risk of turning to crime positive alternatives such as sports, arts, mentoring, and employment training.
Paragraph D
Urban design also plays an important role in city safety. The concept of "designing out crime" involves planning public spaces, buildings, and neighbourhoods in ways that naturally reduce the opportunity for criminal activity. Well-lit, open public spaces with good sight lines — where people can clearly see what is happening around them — feel safer and are less likely to be used for criminal activity. Busy streets and ground-floor shops that keep people active in public spaces at all times of day and evening create a natural form of safety sometimes called "eyes on the street." Conversely, areas with abandoned buildings, dark alleyways, and poor maintenance tend to attract criminal activity. Cities that invest in the physical quality of public spaces and ensure that areas are well maintained and actively used tend to have lower crime rates.

Passage 8: Waste Management — Dealing with What We Throw Away
Topic: Urban Environment  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Every day, cities around the world produce enormous amounts of waste — packaging, food waste, old electronics, paper, glass, plastic, and much more. Managing this waste — collecting it, transporting it, and disposing of it safely — is one of the most important practical challenges that city governments face. If waste is not managed well, it piles up in the streets, creates health hazards, pollutes water and soil, and contributes to environmental problems. As cities grow and people consume more goods, the amount of waste they produce also increases. Finding more sustainable ways to manage waste is now a priority for cities around the world.
Paragraph B
The traditional approach to dealing with waste was simply to bury it in large pits in the ground, called "landfill sites." Landfill is cheap and simple, but it has serious environmental problems. Decomposing waste in landfill produces methane — a powerful greenhouse gas that contributes to climate change. Chemicals from waste can also leak into the soil and contaminate groundwater. Landfill sites take up a lot of land, and in many densely populated areas, there is simply no space left to build new ones. Another approach is incineration — burning waste at very high temperatures. Incineration reduces the volume of waste significantly and can be used to generate electricity. However, it produces air pollutants and releases carbon dioxide, contributing to climate change.
Paragraph C
Recycling — collecting and reprocessing materials so they can be used again — is a much more environmentally friendly approach to waste management. Many cities encourage residents to separate their waste into different categories — paper, glass, plastic, metal, and food waste — so that each material can be collected and recycled separately. Recycling reduces the need to extract new raw materials, saves energy, and reduces the amount of waste sent to landfill or incineration. Some materials — such as glass and metal — can be recycled indefinitely without losing quality. However, recycling is not always simple or cheap, and some materials — particularly certain types of plastic — are difficult or expensive to recycle effectively. Contamination — when the wrong materials are put in recycling bins — also makes recycling less effective.
Paragraph D
Many experts argue that recycling, while important, is not enough on its own. The best approach is to produce less waste in the first place. This means designing products that last longer, can be repaired, and use less packaging. Some cities have introduced regulations to reduce unnecessary packaging — for example, banning single-use plastic bags and encouraging businesses to use packaging made from recycled or biodegradable materials. The concept of the "circular economy" takes this idea further — it involves designing products and systems so that materials are always reused or recycled, and nothing is wasted. Composting — converting food and garden waste into fertiliser for soil — is also an important way to deal with organic waste sustainably. Educating the public about waste reduction and recycling is essential for making any of these approaches work effectively.

Passage 9: Gender Equality — Progress and Challenges
Topic: Society & Equality  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Gender equality means that all people — regardless of whether they are male, female, or non-binary — have the same rights, opportunities, and treatment in society. Over the past hundred years, there has been enormous progress towards gender equality in many parts of the world. In the early twentieth century, women in most countries did not have the right to vote, were not allowed to own property in their own name, and had very limited access to education or professional careers. Today, women in many countries can vote, own property, access education at all levels, and work in virtually any profession. However, significant inequalities still exist in almost every society, and achieving true gender equality remains an ongoing challenge.
Paragraph B
One of the most visible signs of gender inequality today is the "gender pay gap" — the difference in average earnings between men and women. In most countries, women earn less than men on average, even when they do the same or similar work. This happens for several reasons. Women are more likely to work part-time — often because they take on more responsibility for childcare and housework. Women are also more concentrated in lower-paid industries and professions, while men are more often found in higher-paid sectors like finance, engineering, and technology. Even within the same job, women are often paid less than men. Research suggests that part of this gap is caused by discrimination — employers, sometimes unconsciously, valuing the work of men more highly than that of women.
Paragraph C
Another important area of gender inequality is representation in leadership. In most countries, men hold the majority of senior positions in politics, business, and other institutions. Women make up just over a quarter of members of parliament around the world on average. In many countries, the proportion is much lower. The situation is similar in business — very few of the world's largest companies are led by women. This is sometimes called the "glass ceiling" — an invisible barrier that makes it harder for women to reach the top positions in their careers. Research suggests that organisations with more diverse leadership — including more women in senior roles — tend to perform better and make better decisions. Many countries and companies have introduced targets or quotas to increase the number of women in leadership positions.
Paragraph D
Progress towards gender equality requires action at many levels. Governments can introduce laws to prevent discrimination and ensure equal pay for equal work. Paid parental leave — time off work for both mothers and fathers after having a child — helps to share childcare responsibilities more equally between parents. Education plays a crucial role — teaching children from an early age that all people deserve equal respect and opportunities, regardless of gender, helps to build fairer societies. Changing attitudes and challenging stereotypes about gender roles is also important — for example, the idea that certain jobs are only suitable for men, or that caring for children is only a woman's responsibility. Many campaigners argue that true gender equality will benefit not only women but everyone in society, by creating fairer and more productive communities.

Passage 10: Social Media and Society — Connecting or Dividing Us?
Topic: Technology & Society  |  Level: B1 (IELTS 3.5–5.5)
Paragraph A
Social media has become one of the most powerful forces shaping modern society. Platforms like Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, and X allow billions of people to share information, opinions, and experiences instantly with others around the world. Many people check their social media accounts many times a day and use them to stay in touch with friends and family, follow news and events, and express their views on important issues. Social media has given ordinary people a voice and a platform that they never had before. It has allowed social movements to organise and grow quickly, helped businesses reach customers directly, and made it easier than ever to stay connected with people we care about, regardless of distance.
Paragraph B
Social media has brought many benefits to society. It has made information and knowledge more accessible to people everywhere — anyone with a phone and an internet connection can now access news, educational content, and expert advice on almost any topic. It has helped people in isolated or rural communities stay connected with the wider world. It has given marginalised groups — including people with disabilities, members of minority communities, and those living in authoritarian countries — a platform to share their experiences and connect with others. During health emergencies, social media has been used to share public health information rapidly. Fundraising campaigns for charitable causes have raised millions of dollars through social media in a very short time.
Paragraph C
However, social media also has serious negative effects on society. One of the biggest problems is the spread of misinformation — false or misleading information that spreads quickly because people share it without checking whether it is true. False stories about health, politics, and other important topics can cause real harm. During elections, misinformation on social media has been used to try to manipulate public opinion and undermine democratic processes. Social media platforms are designed to keep users engaged for as long as possible, and they often promote content that provokes strong emotions — including anger and outrage — because this keeps people scrolling. This can make social divisions worse by reinforcing existing prejudices and making it harder for people with different views to understand each other.
Paragraph D
Many people are concerned about the effect of social media on mental health, particularly for young people. Research has found that excessive use of social media is linked to higher rates of anxiety, depression, and loneliness among teenagers. Social comparison — judging your own life against the carefully selected and often edited highlights that others share online — can make people feel inadequate and unhappy. Cyberbullying — using social media to bully or harass others — is a serious problem that has affected many young people. Governments and social media companies are facing increasing pressure to do more to protect users, especially children, from these harms. Some countries are now introducing laws to limit how young children can use social media, and to require platforms to be more transparent about how their systems work.
